config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I think society matters a lot, but individual rights should not be treated as secondary by default, because that is how governments and majorities can easily abuse power. In my view, the real goal is to protect both: a healthy society needs rules and shared responsibility, but it also needs strong limits on what the state or the crowd can do to one person. From my perspective, prioritizing society over the individual too easily becomes an excuse to silence dissent and ignore personal dignity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support the idea that society should generally come before the individual, because in the United States we have seen how easily majorities and institutions can trample minority rights when “the common good” is used as a blank check. Society absolutely needs rules, public safety, and shared obligations, but those should be bounded by strong individual rights, not the other way around. I also do not buy the claim that prioritizing the individual automatically means selfishness; protecting personal liberty is often what keeps society honest and prevents abuse of power. If the state can override rights whenever it says society benefits, then rights are not real protections at all.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should take priority over the individual as a general rule, because in practice that idea often gives governments and majorities too much power. In Indonesia, I have seen how easy it is for “public interest” or “social harmony” to be used to pressure people into silence, especially when dissent is inconvenient. Society does need order, mutual responsibility, and limits on harmful behavior, but those should exist to protect people, not to erase their rights. If individual dignity is not protected first, then “society” becomes a vague excuse for control.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support putting society above the individual as a general principle, because in the United States that kind of language has too often been used to justify censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. A society is only legitimate if it protects the rights of the people inside it, including unpopular minorities and dissenters, not just the majority’s comfort or sense of order. That does not mean individual freedom is absolute in every case, since laws against violence, fraud, and public harm are necessary, but those limits should be narrow and justified. To me, the burden should always be on society to prove why a right can be restricted, not on the individual to prove why it should exist.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not accept the statement as a general principle, because in Indonesia I have seen how “society first” can become a polite excuse for pressure, conformity, and abuse by authorities or local majorities. Public order matters, but it should exist to protect people’s dignity and safety, not to make it easier to silence unpopular voices or control personal choices. Rights should be the baseline, and any restriction in the name of society must be narrow, necessary, and openly justified. If that burden is not high, then “society” becomes a mask for power, not a genuine common good.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support the idea that society should take priority over the rights of the individual, because from a United States perspective that is exactly the kind of logic that has been used to excuse segregation, censorship, and other abuses of power. Public safety and shared rules matter, but they only have legitimacy when they are constrained by real rights, especially for minorities and dissenters who cannot rely on majority goodwill. I also reject the false choice that says we must either choose society or the individual; a healthy society depends on individuals having protected freedoms, not on those freedoms being treated as optional. If the burden is not on society to justify every restriction, then rights are just slogans instead of actual limits on power.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
